metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
verify command `wc -l)` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
verify command `wc -l)` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit proving command to AC5, e.g. `python -c 'from rebar.llm.plan_review.det_floor import decomposition_state_block, veto_undecomposed_g5'`, or note that AC6's test_g5_decomp_det.py suite is the oracle.
evidence: AC5 states the requirement but provides no 'verify:' clause
AC6 lists test_g5_decomp_det.py in the named suites, which implicitly covers the re-export — but the link is not made explicit
findings.finding: AC5 (back-compat re-export) has no explicit proving command; it relies implicitly on the test suite in AC6 without stating that connection.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'Back-compat: det_floor still exposes decomposition_state_block and veto_undecomposed_g5 (re-exported); pass1.py and test_g5_decomp_det.py unchanged'
scenarios: An executor who reads AC5 in isolation has no command to run to verify the re-export is present and importable at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC5: '(verify: `python -c "from rebar.llm.plan_review.det_floor import decomposition_state_block, veto_undecomposed_g5"` exits 0, OR covered by AC6 test_g5_decomp_det.py suite)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the expected output (0) for each grep-c call in AC3, e.g. `grep -c det_floor .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` should output `0`.
evidence: 'verify: grep -c det_floor on all three' — no expected count stated
grep -c returns an integer; without '== 0' the check is ambiguous if any residual reference exists
findings.finding: AC3's grep-c proving command omits the expected output value (0), so an executor cannot determine pass vs fail from the command result alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'verify: grep -c det_floor on all three'
scenarios: Executor runs grep -c det_floor .github/module-size-allowlist.txt, gets '1' (a comment mentioning det_floor), and cannot tell if AC is satisfied.
If the arch.md deletion leaves a changelog note containing 'det_floor', grep -c returns non-zero with no stated failure threshold.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC3 verify clause with three explicit assertions:
```
grep -c det_floor .github/module-size-allowlist.txt   # expect 0
grep -c det_floor .github/module-size-ceilings.txt    # expect 0
grep -c det_floor docs/architecture.md                 # expect 0
```
If residual non-entry occurrences (e.g. changelog) are expected, use a stricter pattern: `grep -cP '^\s*det_floor' <file>`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC3's verify step to use `grep -c det_floor <file>; [ $? -eq 1 ]` or rephrase as `! grep -q det_floor <file>` so the command exits 0 on successful removal.
evidence: 'verify: grep -c det_floor on all three'
grep -c returns exit code 1 when match count is 0, which is the desired post-removal state
findings.finding: AC3's verify step `grep -c det_floor on all three` will exit non-zero (exit code 1) when the pattern is absent — i.e., when the removal has succeeded — because grep exits 1 on no-match. An implementer or CI script using this literally would interpret a successful removal as a failure.
scenarios: An implementer writes a CI check `grep -c det_floor .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && echo OK` — it exits 1 on success, silently marking a correct removal as broken.
A reviewer running the verify command sees a non-zero exit and assumes the removal failed.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `verify: grep -c det_floor on all three` with `verify: grep -rn det_floor .github/module-size-allowlist.txt .github/module-size-ceilings.txt docs/architecture.md | wc -l  # expect 0` or `! grep -q det_floor .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && ! grep -q det_floor .github/module-size-ceilings.txt && ! grep -q det_floor docs/architecture.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/plan-review-gate.md` to the edit set and update its references to `det_floor.py` to reflect that the module is no longer size-ceilinged after this ticket.
evidence: docs/plan-review-gate.md:63: `det_floor.py`. The *only* tier
docs/plan-review-gate.md:74: `det_floor` to keep that size-ceilinged module within its ratchet
Plan edit set lists only: .github/module-size-allowlist.txt, .github/module-size-ceilings.txt, docs/architecture.md — docs/plan-review-gate.md is absent
findings.finding: docs/plan-review-gate.md references det_floor.py by name (line 63: 'det_floor.py. The *only* tier...'; line 74: 'det_floor to keep that size-ceilinged module...') but is not enumerated in the plan's edit set. After this ticket lands, det_floor.py is no longer size-ceilinged, making those references stale.
findings.location: What section / edit set; docs/plan-review-gate.md lines 63 and 74
scenarios: A reader of plan-review-gate.md after this ticket lands sees a stale description implying det_floor.py is still size-ceilinged.
A future contributor relying on the docs to understand the module structure gets an outdated picture.
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/plan-review-gate.md, update the lines that describe det_floor.py as size-ceilinged to simply reference it as a module without the ceiling qualifier, or briefly note the extraction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/operator_attested_eval.json` to AC3's verification scope and update its `detector` field from `rebar.llm.plan_review.det_floor._operator_evidence_ac_gaps` to `rebar.llm.plan_review.det_operator_attested.operator_evidence_ac_gaps` (the already-correct import in the harness).
evidence: operator_attested_eval.json line 5: `"detector": "rebar.llm.plan_review.det_floor._operator_evidence_ac_gaps"`
operator_attested_eval.py lines 38-41 already import from `det_operator_attested` (the correct current location)

AC3 verifies: `grep -c det_floor` on `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt`, `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt`, `docs/architecture.md` — but not on `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/operator_attested_eval.json`
Plan states it moves symbols OUT of det_floor — the stale metadata record becomes a referenced-but-wrong module path after this refactor completes
findings.finding: The committed eval-result metadata in `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/operator_attested_eval.json` records `"detector": "rebar.llm.plan_review.det_floor._operator_evidence_ac_gaps"`, but that function already lives in `det_operator_attested` (not `det_floor`). The plan's AC3 verification step (`grep -c det_floor` on the three config files) does not include this eval artifact, so an executor following the plan to completion will not detect or update this stale module-path reference. If `_operator_evidence_ac_gaps` (or related symbols) were ever moved again, or if the harness reproducibility check is re-run and compares against the committed metadata, the stale path could mislead a debugger or automated tooling that treats the `detector` field as an importable path.
scenarios: An executor runs the plan's AC3 grep checks, finds them clean, and ships — leaving the stale `detector` path in the eval JSON unreported.

A future engineer re-runs the operator-attested harness with `--rebuild` and finds the `detector` field points to a non-existent symbol in `det_floor`, causing confusion about where the canonical detector lives.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, extend the grep check to include `runs/operator_attested_eval.json` and add a step: `sed -i 's/det_floor\._operator_evidence_ac_gaps/det_operator_attested.operator_evidence_ac_gaps/' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/operator_attested_eval.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `_find_cycle` should be replaced with `graphlib.TopologicalSorter` + `CycleError` at relocation time, since `graphlib` is already the adopted stdlib primitive for DAG cycle detection in three other modules (lint_refs.py, interpreter.py, executor.py), or document why the bespoke DFS is intentionally retained (e.g., needing the cycle path, not just the signal).
evidence: src/rebar/llm/workflow/lint_refs.py:26: import graphlib
src/rebar/llm/workflow/lint_refs.py:94: order = list(graphlib.TopologicalSorter(graph).static_order())
src/rebar/llm/workflow/lint_refs.py:94: except graphlib.CycleError:
src/rebar/llm/workflow/interpreter.py:279: return list(graphlib.TopologicalSorter(graph).static_order())
src/rebar/llm/workflow/executor.py:457: return list(graphlib.TopologicalSorter(graph).static_order())
det_floor.py:513-538: hand-rolled DFS _find_cycle with WHITE/GRAY/BLACK coloring
Plan states 'no signature/logic change; symbols relocated' — but relocation is the cheapest moment to consolidate
findings.finding: The plan relocates _find_cycle (a hand-rolled DFS cycle detector) into det_lint.py, but the codebase already uses stdlib `graphlib.TopologicalSorter` + `CycleError` for the same problem class in three other locations (lint_refs.py:94, interpreter.py:279, executor.py:457). Relocation is the natural moment to replace the bespoke implementation with the already-used stdlib primitive, eliminating the only remaining bespoke cycle-detector in the tree.
scenarios: If a future contributor finds two cycle-detection implementations (graphlib-based in workflow/, DFS-based in det_lint.py) they must maintain both and cannot know which is canonical.
If graphlib's CycleError path ever returns a richer error (path information), the bespoke version would miss the improvement.
findings.suggested_fix: If the cycle PATH (not just the signal) is needed by p5_task_dag's finding evidence, retain the DFS but add a comment noting the intentional divergence from graphlib. If only the signal is needed, replace _find_cycle with a graphlib.TopologicalSorter call at relocation time and drop the bespoke implementation.
